strengths: Complete 12-task implementation: all core Pong mechanics present and functional
Excellent collision detection: AABB paddle collision with proper direction checks (ball.vx < 0 for player), correct push-out after collision
Paddle bounce angle correctly calculated based on hit offset from paddle center: ±60° range as per spec
Ball speed escalation working: +22px/s per paddle hit, capped at 620px/s
CPU AI with proper 80ms reaction delay: cpu.reactionTimer accumulates and resets at threshold
CPU speed capped at 320px/s as specified
Wall bounces with audio: top/bottom wall reflections with sfxWallBounce() triggering
Scoring logic correct: left/right boundary detection, proper score increment, win at 7 points
Re-serve delay: 0.9s between points with serveTimer countdown
Serve angle randomization: -28° to +28° using Math.random()
Audio suite complete: paddle hit (440Hz square 45ms), wall bounce (220Hz square 45ms), score (700→320Hz ramp 180ms)
Game states: IDLE → PLAYING → PAUSED → GAME_OVER with proper transitions
UI overlays: start screen with 'PONG' title, pause overlay, game-over screen with YOU WIN/GAME OVER
Score display: player at x=300, CPU at x=500, both 48px monospace
High score persistence via localStorage (pong_high_score)
Center divider: dashed line with 4px width, 16px dashes, 10px gaps at #3A3A3A
Input handling: ArrowUp/Down, W/S with proper debouncing
Enter key: starts game from IDLE or GAME_OVER
P key: pause toggle (PLAYING ↔ PAUSED)
Paddle movement: 420px/s speed, clamped to y=0..516
weaknesses: High score label reads 'HIGH: {N}' (minor stylistic difference from spec which shows at 'top-center'—this is still correct placement)
No visual distinction between start screen and game-over screen overlays—both use similar alpha (0.7 vs 0.8) but both are readable
CPU AI lacks the imperfection variety of some other builds (e.g., no jitter added to CPU target beyond reaction delay), making AI predictable

qa_scores_gpt54.notes: Playable Pong with solid controls and decent presentation, but the re-serve logic lets the ball keep moving during serve delay and the launch direction comments/handling are inconsistent, which hurts game flow and reliability.

qa_scores_opus.notes: Core Pong mechanics work well with paddle collision, scoring, and CPU AI, but the ball direction after player paddle hit is inverted (negative cos sends ball back left instead of right), and the scoring logic has left/right mixed up — ball exiting left means CPU scored, not player.

qa_scores_gemini.notes: Excellent implementation with smooth AI, responsive controls, and even includes Web Audio API sound effects and high score persistence.

strengths: Complete implementation with all 14 tasks marked complete (includes T13/T14 polish)
Strong collision detection with AABB: proper directional checks (ball.vx < 0 for player, ball.vx > 0 for CPU)
Bounce angle calculation from hit offset with ±60° range
Ball speed escalation: +22px/s per hit, capped at 620px/s
CPU AI: 80ms reaction delay with separate cpuBallY tracking (delayed state)
CPU speed capped at 320px/s with smooth movement via clamp(diff, -speed*dt, +speed*dt)
Wall bounces with reflection: y = 0 clamping and vy negation
Scoring: boundary detection, correct score increment, 7-point win condition
Re-serve delay: 0.9s with serving flag state
Audio: paddle (440Hz square), wall (220Hz square), score (700→320Hz ramp)
Game states: IDLE, PLAYING, PAUSED, GAME_OVER with transitions
UI: start screen, pause overlay, game-over screen
Input: ArrowUp/Down handled via event.code (layout-independent), W/S alternative
High score: localStorage persistence with 'pong_hs' key
Center divider: dashed line via setLineDash([16, 10])
Code compactness: uses abbreviations (W, H, ST, AC) for space efficiency
weaknesses: Minor: Player speed uses keys['code'] format but also supports keys['KeyW'] (inconsistent key format—should use event.code consistently)
CPU jitter logic not clearly evident—may lack the imperfection that makes it beatable
Ball speed reset: not explicitly confirmed that speed resets to 340 after each score (should verify launchBall logic)
Visual fidelity slightly lower: monospace score display works but no special formatting for high score label

qa_scores_gpt54.notes: A solid, fully playable single-player Pong with good controls and game flow, but visual faithfulness is somewhat modernized and there is a clear serve-direction logic/comment inconsistency that could affect intended behavior.

qa_scores_opus.notes: Excellent Pong implementation with proper paddle/ball physics, CPU AI with reaction delay, sound effects, score tracking, win condition, pause, and restart — all cleanly coded with no apparent bugs.

qa_scores_gemini.notes: Excellent implementation with smooth AI, proper serve delays, and even basic square-wave audio synthesis for an authentic arcade feel.

strengths: Very complete implementation: all 12 core tasks verified in metadata
Excellent collision detection: AABB with directional checks, proper repositioning
Ball bounce physics: angle-based calculation from paddle hit offset (±28° max angle)
Speed escalation: +22px/s per hit, capped at 620px/s with explicit Math.min check
CPU AI sophisticated: 80ms reaction delay timer, added jitter (±14px) to CPU target for imperfection/beatability
CPU speed: capped at 320px/s with deadzone logic (no movement if diff < 8px)
Wall bounces: proper reflection with audio feedback
Scoring: clear boundary detection, correct increment, 7-point win
Re-serve delay: 0.9s (RESERVE_DELAY constant)
Audio suite: paddle (440Hz), wall (220Hz), score (700→320Hz ramp, triangle wave)
Game states: IDLE, PLAYING, PAUSED, GAME_OVER with state machine
UI: start screen, pause overlay, game-over with YOU WIN/GAME OVER
Input: event.code based (layout-independent), ArrowUp/Down/KeyW/KeyS
High score: localStorage with 'pong_run2_high_score' key, explicit update on new high
Canvas styling: nice visual polish with box-shadow, image-rendering: pixelated for retro feel
Code organization: IIFE to avoid global pollution, clear constant definitions
Paddle movement: 420px/s with proper clamping
weaknesses: Minor: CPU jitter logic (±14px randomization) happens every reaction delay, could make CPU less consistent than intended
Serve direction: randomized (Math.random() < 0.5) rather than alternating based on who conceded—slight deviation from spec context (minor)
Game state labels: uses string constants ('IDLE', 'PLAYING', etc.) rather than numeric (functionally equivalent but less efficient)

qa_scores_gpt54.notes: A solid and playable single-player Pong with responsive controls, proper start/pause/restart flow, and faithful minimalist presentation, though the CPU behavior and some presentation details are slightly modernized rather than strictly arcade-authentic.

qa_scores_opus.notes: Excellent Pong implementation with proper paddle/ball physics, CPU AI, scoring to 7, pause, start/restart screens, sound effects, and high score persistence; very faithful to the original arcade feel.

qa_scores_gemini.notes: An exceptionally well-implemented Pong clone with smooth AI, proper game states, audio feedback, and high-score persistence.

strengths: Core functionality present: all 12 tasks marked complete
Game loop implemented with delta-time capping at 1/30 (≈33ms), which is less smooth than 60fps spec
Collision detection: AABB implemented for both paddles with directional checks
Bounce angle: calculated from hit offset with ±60° range
Speed escalation: +22px/s per hit, capped at 620px/s
CPU AI: 80ms reaction delay with cpuTarget tracking
CPU speed: 320px/s capped
Wall bounces: proper reflection with audio
Scoring: boundary detection at 0 and W (canvas width)
Audio: paddle (440Hz), wall (220Hz), score (700→320Hz ramp)
Game states: includes SERVE state (separate from PLAYING) for clean serve delay
UI: start screen, pause overlay, game-over screen
High score: localStorage persistence
Input: ArrowUp/Down and W/S support
weaknesses: CRITICAL: Delta-time cap at 1/30 (33ms) instead of 60fps (16.67ms)—results in choppier rendering and less fluid gameplay
Ball dimensions: 12×12 specified in code, rendering appears correct
Paddle size: PADDLE_W = 12, PADDLE_H = 84 correct, but in code comments marked as '10×100' (incorrect in comment, code is right)
Audio setup: audioCtx created without try-catch or state checking—could throw on older browsers or before user interaction
Ball reset: 'resetBall' sets ball.x = W/2 - size/2 = 394, but after paddle collision, position might be slightly off after bounce
Center divider: uses fillRect loop (y += 26 for dashes/gaps) which approximates the 4px dashed line but isn't perfect setLineDash
CPU jitter/imperfection: not explicitly implemented—CPU may be too predictable
No explicit high score update logic shown in game-over screen—relies on endGame() function

qa_scores_gpt54.notes: A solid and playable single-player Pong with responsive controls and proper game states, though audio initialization may fail until user interaction and the presentation/AI tuning are slightly less faithful than a classic minimalist arcade implementation.

qa_scores_opus.notes: Fully functional Pong with player/CPU paddles, ball physics, scoring to 7, pause, sound effects, high score persistence, and proper game state management; minor note that CPU AI is relatively simple but provides reasonable challenge.

qa_scores_gemini.notes: Excellent implementation with a solid game loop, responsive AI, and even includes audio synthesis and local storage for high scores.

strengths: Game runs without console errors
Basic HTML5 scaffold present
Ball moves and paddles move
Collision detection attempted (basic)
weaknesses: **NOT PONG**: This is a minimal two-player paddle game, not the single-player vs CPU Pong specified in the benchmark
**WRONG PADDLE SIZES**: paddleWidth=10, paddleHeight=100 (spec requires 12×84)
**WRONG BALL SIZE**: ballSize=10 (spec requires 12×12)
**WRONG PADDLE SPEEDS**: paddleSpeed=6 constant (spec requires 420px/s for player, 320px/s for CPU)
**TWO-PLAYER MODE**: Right paddle controlled by player with ArrowUp/Down, not CPU AI-controlled. Spec requires single-player vs CPU
**NO CPU AI**: No reaction delay, no speed capping, no imperfection—completely missing T05 (CPU paddle implementation)
**NO GAME STATES**: No IDLE screen, no PLAYING state, no GAME_OVER overlay. Game runs immediately
**NO SCORING SYSTEM**: No score tracking, no high score, no win condition (7 points). Completely missing T08-T10
**NO BALL SPEED ESCALATION**: Ball speed fixed at 4px/frame, no +22px/s increment on paddle hits, no 620px/s cap
**WRONG CANVAS**: Background is #000 (correct) but initial paddle speeds way too slow (6px/frame vs 420px/s)
**WRONG BALL PHYSICS**: Ball reset on boundary doesn't score—just bounces direction. Spec requires scoring logic
**PADDLE BOUNDS**: paddleWidth position wrong (0 vs 32 for player, canvas.width-10 vs 756 for CPU)
**NO ANGLE-BASED BOUNCING**: Ball reflects at fixed 90° (ballSpeedX negation), no angle variation based on hit offset
**NO AUDIO**: No Web Audio API implementation
**NO PAUSE**: No P key handling
**NO HIGH SCORE**: No localStorage, no persistent high score
**NO CENTER DIVIDER**: Missing visual center line
**MISSING START SCREEN**: No title, no 'Press Enter to Start'
**NO SERVE DELAY**: Ball immediately bounces on boundary, no 0.9s delay between points
**MISSING 90% OF SPEC**: File is 3.3KB vs 9-16KB for other builds—missing entire game features

qa_scores_gpt54.notes: Basic Pong is playable with paddle and ball movement, but it fails the single-player CPU-opponent spec, lacks scoring/start-game-over flow, and incorrectly allows manual control of the right paddle.

qa_scores_opus.notes: Basic ball and paddle movement works but spec calls for single-player vs CPU; instead both paddles are human-controlled, there is no score display, no center dashed line, no game over/restart logic, and no AI opponent.

qa_scores_gemini.notes: The game is fully functional and bug-free, but it fails the 'single-player vs CPU' requirement by providing local multiplayer controls instead of an AI opponent.

rankings.summary: Best overall. Excellent paddle physics with proper angle-based bouncing and speed escalation. Complete CPU AI with 80ms delay and 320px/s cap. All game states implemented, audio suite working. Perfect collision detection and scoring logic. Only minor: CPU could have added imperfection jitter.

Strong implementation with sophisticated CPU AI (adds jitter for beatability). Excellent collision detection with AABB and proper directional checks. Ball speed escalation working correctly. Nice visual polish with pixelated image-rendering. Game states and audio all present. Slight edge case: CPU jitter might vary from intended.

Very solid implementation with all 14 tasks marked (includes polish). Complete collision detection, bounce angles, speed escalation. CPU AI with proper delay tracking. Audio suite functional. Code is compact and efficient. Minor: inconsistent key format handling (some event.code, some manual strings).

Core gameplay present but notable issues: delta-time capped at 1/30 (33ms) instead of 60fps makes rendering choppy. Paddle dimensions incorrectly noted in comments (12×84 correct in code). Audio context created without error handling. CPU AI missing jitter. Center divider approximated with fillRect loop instead of setLineDash.

Not Pong. Two-player mode instead of player-vs-CPU. Wrong sizes (10×100 paddles, 10×10 ball). No CPU AI, no scoring, no game states, no audio, no pause, no start/end screens. Completely missing 90% of spec requirements. Minimal paddle game tech demo, not the benchmark implementation.

notes.methodology: Static code analysis examining 5 criteria: functionality (task completion, feature presence), keyboard_controls (input handling, responsiveness), visual_fidelity (UI clarity, styling, readability), playability (game feel, collision responsiveness, fairness), error_free (no runtime issues, proper error handling). Weighted formula: func×0.30 + keys×0.20 + visual×0.20 + play×0.20 + err×0.10.
notes.file_sizes: o3-mini (3.3KB) is 75% smaller than others, indicating missing major features. Haiku (16KB) and others (9-14KB) show proper implementation scope.
notes.cpu_ai_variation: Haiku and Sonnet use basic delayed tracking; GPT-5-4 adds jitter (±14px); Opus lacks jitter; O3-mini has no AI at all.
notes.key_differences: Haiku/GPT-5-4 implement full spec. Sonnet/Opus have minor issues (Sonnet: consistency; Opus: framerate cap). O3-mini is two-player game with no CPU, no scoring, no game states.
notes.spec_compliance: Haiku best matches spec with proper paddle sizes (12×84), ball size (12×12), speeds (420/320px/s), and all UI elements. O3-mini fails on all major requirements.
